Dear Mayor Gonzales: 
In your letter of September 28, 1979, you inquire about 
a matter arising under the disqualification provisions (Govern-
ment Code Sections 87100, et ~.) of the Political Reform 
Act ("the Act"). 
Because, under the Act, opinions and advice 
are issued only in response to inquiries from persons, or on 
behalf of persons, whose duties are the subject of the inquiries, 
your request for a formal opinion in this matter is denied. 
The following discussion is provided for information only. 
You ask specifically whether a city councilmember should 
be disqualified from participation in city council personnel 
actions concerning a discharged city employee who had con-
tributed to the councilmember's 1978 campaign committee. 
Government Code Section 87100 prohibits any public 
official at any level of state or local government from 
making, participating in the making or in any way attempting 
to use his official position to influence a governmental 
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest. 
Government Code Section 87103 states that a public 
official has "financial interest" in a decision if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the 
public generally, on: 
"(c) any source of income .... " 
The terms of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 
are substantially incorporated by Section 4 of City of Colton 
Resolution No. 3623, adopting a conflict of interest code 
applicable to members of the city council pursuant to the 
Political Reform Act of 1974. 
• 


Honorable Frank A. Gonzales, Mayor 
October 19, 1979 
Page Two 
"Income" is specifically defined by Government Code 
Section 82030(b)(1) to exclude campaign contributions required 
to be reported under Chapter 4 of the Act. 
Assuming that the employee's contribution to the council-
man is a contribution within the meaning of the Act, it 
would not constitute "income" to the councilmember. 
Accordingly, 
the Act would not require the councilmember to disqualify 
himself from participating in decisions affecting the employee. 
Even though the councilmember would not be required to 
disqualify himself under the Act, please be advised that the 
Supreme Court is presently considering the question whether 
city councilmembers' participation in a zoning decision 
directly affecting contributors to their campaigns may be 
said to create an appearance of bias sufficient to provide a 
basis for overturning a decision under Code of Civil Procedure 
1094.5. 
Woodland Hills Residents Ass'n., Inc., et ale v. 
Los Angeles City Council (L.A. No. 31133).

Acting City Manager Elliott stated on August 15, 1979, the Civil Service Board held

The findings have been 
fi 1 ed and certifi ed cop i es have been deli vered to the t~ayor and City Counc 11, as 
per Rule XV, Section 5, of the City's Rules and Regulations. 
Mr. Elliott said this matter is being continued from a previous Council Meeting 
at the request of Mr. Young. 
Mayor Gonzales stated the City Council had requested the Civil, Service Board to 
hold a hearing and make recommendations as to the discipline imposed on Jerry 
Young, and the Board did hold such a hearing. 
The findings of the Board were 
that the conduct of Mr. Young was insubordinate, that discipline was justified, 
and the termination was reasonable. 
Mayor Gonzales advised that the City Council has rece-ived a petition containing 
approximately 203 signatures in support of Mr. Young, which he presented to 
City Clerk Ramos for the record. 
Mayor Gonzales asked Mr. Young if he wished to make a statement. 
Mr. Young replied from the audience that he had no comments to make.

Councilman Rehrer stated that although he felt the Civil Service Board had done 
a tremendous job in considering this matter, he did not go along with the Board's 
decision, because there were many discrepancies involved since that time, and he 
did not feel that Mr. Young should be penalized, therefore, he would make a motion 
to reinstate Mr. Young to the position of City Auditor under the direction of the 
City Manager's Office. 
Motion seconded by Councilwoman Cisneros. 
Mayor Gonzales stated that before the vote, he vJOuld like to clarify the validity 
of the motion with the City Attorney, inasmuch as the position of City Auditor 
had been deleted from the budget.

position of City Auditor Vias still on the books and a part of the Table of Organi-

position of City Auditor had been dropped from the proposed City Manager's Office

Councilman Rehrer contended although this may have been deleted from the City

Mayor Gonzales read a portion of the Budget Report, indicating that the position 
of City Auditor had been deleted, and the position of Auditor/Anaylst would be 
changed to Accounting Supervisor, pending a public hearing for this change. 
Discussion then followed between Mayor Gonzales and Councilman Rehrer with regard

Councilman Temby stated he would second the motion, and would also like to direct

Acting City Manager Elliott stated on August 15, 1979, the Civil Service Board held 
a special meeting to consider the Appeal of Jerry Young. 
The findings have been

per Rule XV, Section 5, of the City's Rules and Regulations. 
Mr. Elliott said this matter is being continued from a previous Council Meeting 
at the request of Mr. Young. 
Mayor Gonzales stated the City Council had requested the Civil, Service Board to 
hold a hearing and make recommendations as to the discipline imposed on Jerry 
Young, and the Board did hold such a hearing. 
The findings of the Board were 
that the conduct of Mr. Young was insubordinate, that discipline was justified, 
and the termination was reasonable. 
Mayor Gonzales advised that the City Council has rece-ived a petition containing 
approximately 203 signatures in support of Mr. Young, which he presented to 
City Clerk Ramos for the record. 
Mayor Gonzales asked Mr. Young if he wished to make a statement. 
Mr. Young replied from the audience that he had no comments to make.

Councilman Rehrer stated that although he felt the Civil Service Board had done 
a tremendous job in considering this matter, he did not go along with the Board's 
decision, because there were many discrepancies involved since that time, and he 
did not feel that Mr. Young should be penalized, therefore, he would make a motion 
to reinstate Mr. Young to the position of City Auditor under the direction of the 
City Manager's Office. 
Motion seconded by Councilwoman Cisneros. 
Mayor Gonzales stated that before the vote, he vJOuld like to clarify the validity 
of the motion with the City Attorney, inasmuch as the position of City Auditor 
had been deleted from the budget. 
Councilman Rehrer questioned when the position had been deleted, and contended the 
position of City Auditor Vias still on the books and a part of the Table of Organi-
zation.

to the powers and duti es of the City r'1anager as Personnel Off; cer concern; ng per-
sonnel matters.

position of City Auditor had been dropped from the proposed City Manager's Office 
budget, and this was decided by the City Council.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilman Leonard Temby must disqualify himself from voting on personnel matters affecting employee Jerry Young, who had contributed to Temby's 1978 campaign committee.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Campaign contributions are statutorily excluded from the definition of 'income' under the Political Reform Act, so the councilmember is not required to disqualify himself based on the Act's conflict of interest provisions. However, the FPPC notes that California courts are considering whether such participation creates an appearance of bias.

The FPPC response clearly states it is providing information only, not a formal opinion, due to standing requirements under the Act.

Gonzales, Mayor City of Colton Colton Civic Center 650 North La Cadena Drive Colton, CA 92324 Dear Mayor Gonzales: In your letter of September 28, 1979, you inquire about a matter arising under the disqualification provisions (Govern- ment Code Sections 87100, et ~.) of the Political Reform Act ("the Act"). Because, under the Act, opinions and advice are issued only in response to inquiries from persons, or on behalf of persons, whose duties are the subject of the inquiries, your request for a formal opinion in this matter is denied. The following discussion is provided for information only. You ask specifically whether a city councilmember should be disqualified from participation in city council personnel actions concerning a discharged city employee who had con- tributed to the councilmember's 1978 campaign committee. Government Code Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any level of state or local government from making, participating in the making or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87103 states that a public official has "financial interest" in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: "(c) any source of income .... " The terms of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 are substantially incorporated by Section 4 of City of Colton Resolution No. 3623, adopting a conflict of interest code applicable to members of the city council pursuant to the Political Reform Act of 1974. • Honorable Frank A. Gonzales, Mayor October 19, 1979 Page Two "Income" is specifically defined by Government Code Section 82030(b)(1) to exclude campaign contributions required to be reported under Chapter 4 of the Act. Assuming that the employee's contribution to the council- man is a contribution within the meaning of the Act, it would not constitute "income" to the councilmember. Accordingly, the Act would not require the councilmember to disqualify himself from participating in decisions affecting the employee. Even though the councilmember would not be required to disqualify himself under the Act, please be advised that the Supreme Court is presently considering the question whether city councilmembers' participation in a zoning decision directly affecting contributors to their campaigns may be said to create an appearance of bias sufficient to provide a basis for overturning a decision under Code of Civil Procedure 1094.5. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Mayor Frank Gonzales of Colton regarding whether Councilman Temby should disqualify himself from voting on personnel matters affecting employee Jerry Young, a campaign contributor. The FPPC concludes that campaign contributions are excluded from the statutory definition of income, so disqualification is not required under the Political Reform Act, but notes pending Supreme Court consideration of appearance of bias issues.
